sections.title: Definitions for the Schedule
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in the Schedule, including 'effective date' as the date set by the Minister for the Act's operation, 'previous Act' as the financial sector law designated by the Authority, and 'previously licensed financial institution' as one licensed under the previous Act. It also specifies that references to numbered items in the Schedule refer to corresponding items within the same Schedule.

requirements.text: Effective date means the date fixed by the Minister in accordance with section 118(1) as the date the Act comes into operation
Previous Act means the financial sector law listed in Schedule 2 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act for which the Authority is the responsible authority
Previously licensed financial institution means a financial institution licensed or deemed licensed under the previous Act
A reference in this Schedule to an item or sub-item by number refers to the corresponding item or sub-item of this Schedule

sections.source_quote: In this Schedule, unless the context indicates otherwise— ''effective date'' means the date fixed by the Minister in accordance with section 118(1) as the date that this Act comes into operation; ''previous Act'' means the financial sector law listed in Schedule 2 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act for which the Authority is the responsible authority; and ''previously licensed financial institution'' means a financial institution that is licensed or deemed to have been licensed under the previous Act.

The definitions are cross-referenced with other acts to ensure consistency in interpretation.

sections.title: Application to public sector pension funds
sections.summary: The Act applies to all public sector pension funds. Unregistered pension funds must apply for registration within 12 months of commencement. Legislation establishing pension funds must be amended within 18 months to align with the Act. Pension fund rules must also be amended within 18 months to align with the Act. New pension funds must comply with the Act and their rules.

Unregistered pension funds must apply for registration within 12 months of commencement
Legislation establishing pension funds must be amended within 18 months to align with the Act

New pension funds must comply with the Act and their rules

sections.title: Curator appointment for insurers
sections.summary: This section amends section 54 to allow the Prudential Authority to appoint a curator for an insurer or controlling company without court intervention. The curator may be provisionally appointed with specific conditions and a rule nisi for confirmation. The court may suspend legal proceedings, grant investigation powers, and set remuneration. The curator acts under Prudential Authority control and must report to them. Creditors and clients retain their rights. The appointment lapses if the Prudential Authority withdraws the letter of appointment or if the court cancels it. The curator may apply to the Prudential Authority for instructions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a curator mechanism for insurers, allowing the Prudential Authority to appoint a curator without court intervention. The curator must report to the Prudential Authority and act under their control. This impacts data transfer DTIAs by creating a governance structure for financial institutions during insolvency or crisis.

The curator may be provisionally appointed with specific conditions and a rule nisi for confirmation
The court may suspend legal proceedings against the insurer during curatorship
The Prudential Authority may investigate the affairs of the insurer or related entities
The curator must report to the Prudential Authority on matters related to business management
The curator's rights do not affect creditors or clients
The appointment lapses if the Prudential Authority withdraws the letter of appointment
The appointment lapses if the court cancels it

sections.title: Application of the Act to financial institutions
sections.summary: This section specifies the application of the Financial Conduct of Financial Institutions Bill to financial institutions, including the requirement that financial institutions providing financial products and services must be licensed under this Act. It also states that the Act applies to all supervised entities, including those that are not financial institutions, and imposes requirements relating to the licensing of financial institutions, supervised entities, the licensing of certain persons related to financial institutions, and the financial products and services provided by financial institutions.

requirements.text: Financial institutions that provide financial products and financial services are subject to this Act and must be licensed under this Act

This Act imposes requirements relating to the licensing of financial institutions and the authorisation of their activities
This Act imposes requirements relating to supervised entities, in order to promote the achievement of the object of this Act
This Act imposes requirements relating to supervised entities, in order to promote the achievement of the purposes set out in Chapters 3 to 10 of this Act
This Act imposes requirements relating to the licensing, approval, notification, registration and conduct of certain persons related to financial institutions

This Act imposes requirements relating to the financial products and financial services provided by financial institutions, the activities performed by financial institutions, and their conduct in relation to the provision of those financial products and financial services and the performance of those activities

sections.title: Amendment of section 126 to require concurrence for licensing actions
sections.summary: This section amends section 126 to mandate that the responsible authority cannot take actions affecting systemically important financial institutions or system operators without concurrence from the other financial sector regulator and the Reserve Bank. The actions include issuing, varying, suspending, or revoking licenses, or granting exemptions under financial sector laws.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires concurrence from multiple regulators before critical financial actions involving systemically important institutions, impacting cross-border data transfers if such institutions handle international payments.

requirements.text: The responsible authority may not take actions specified in subsection (3) for systemically important financial institutions or system operators without concurrence from the other financial sector regulator
The Reserve Bank must concurrence if the action affects a systemically important financial institution

sections.source_quote: The responsible authority may not take any of the actions specified in subsection (2) in respect of a systemically important financial institution or a system operator or other participant in systemically important payment system unless (a) the other financial sector regulator has concurred and (b) if the action relates to or affects a systemically important financial institution, the Reserve Bank has concurred.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for financial sector actions

sections.title: Professional custodian services for financial assets
sections.summary: This section defines professional custodian services as holding assets in custody on behalf of financial customers as a business or part of a business. It specifies that this activity relates to the current FAIS 'administrative FSP' (LISP) category where the distinguishing feature is 'bulking', but notes that further consideration is being given to whether other forms of investment platforms require licensing. The Authority has regulatory authority over this activity under section 291 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines custodial services for financial assets as a licensed activity under FAIS, relevant for DTIA when assessing custody operations across borders.

sections.title: Conversion of previously licensed financial institutions
sections.summary: This section mandates that previously licensed financial institutions continue operating under the previous Act until their licenses are converted to this Act's licenses within two years. The Authority must convert licenses within two years, publish a conversion process within two months, and ensure the conversion process is reasonable and fair with sufficient engagement. It also specifies that institutions must be converted to activities similar to their previous business or make arrangements to discharge obligations, ensure orderly resolution, or transfer business if they do not meet the criteria.

requirements.text: Every previously licensed financial institution continues to exist as a licensed financial institution under the previous Act until its license is converted to this Act's license within two years
The Authority must convert all previously licensed financial institutions' licenses to this Act within two years
The Authority must publish the conversion process within two months of the effective date
The conversion process must be reasonable and fair
The conversion process must allow for sufficient engagement with previously licensed financial institutions
The Authority must convert a previously licensed financial institution's license to activities similar to those it was licensed for before the effective date
A previously licensed financial institution may only be issued a license for activities it was permitted to conduct under the previous Act

If the Authority does not convert a license for an activity similar to the institution's previous business, the institution must discharge obligations under contracts, ensure orderly resolution, or transfer business to another financial institution

sections.source_quote: As of the effective date of this Schedule, every previously licensed financial institution that was, immediately before that date, licensed under the previous Act continues to exist as a licensed financial institution in terms of the previous Act, as if it had been licensed under this Act, and may continue to conduct the business for which it was licensed, until its licence under the previous Act is converted to a licence under this Act during the period of two years referred to in sub-item (2), subject to and in accordance with the requirements and obligations imposed under this Act.

sections.title: Underwriting public offerings
sections.summary: This section defines underwriting a public offering as guaranteeing the sale of securities by purchasing them at a stated price from the issuer. It notes that this activity is indirectly regulated under the Financial Markets Act for listed securities and the Companies Act for unlisted securities, with input sought on whether it must be licensed. The underwriting of CIS participatory interests is included in the activity of the provider of a pooled investment.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines underwriting as a licensed activity relevant for DTIA when assessing cross-border securities transactions.

sections.title: Trading and market activities
sections.summary: This section defines trading as buying or selling securities for own account or on behalf of another person as a business, part of a business, or incidental to conducting a business. It also defines making a market as stating prices at which financial instruments can be acquired or disposed of, and clearing services as calculating and determining exact numbers of securities and money for settlement. Settlement services involve completing transactions through a settlement system. The definitions are subject to stakeholder engagement on scope and demarcation.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines trading and related market activities relevant for DTIA when assessing cross-border securities transactions and data flows.

requirements.text: Buying or selling of securities for own account or on behalf of another person as a business, part of a business or incidental to conducting a business
Stating prices at which a person offers to acquire or dispose of financial instruments
Calculating and determining the exact number or nominal value of securities to be transferred by or on behalf of a seller
Settling transactions in securities by effecting transfer of securities and payment of funds through a settlement system
Holding securities or funds in custody on behalf of another person

sections.source_quote: Buying or selling of securities for own account or on behalf of another person as a business, a part of a business or incidental to conducting a business

sections.title: Benchmark and index services
sections.summary: This section defines benchmark services as providing benchmarks used directly or indirectly within financial instruments or contracts, or indices that have no or very few market-led substitutes. It specifies that benchmarks must not cause significant adverse impacts on market integrity, financial stability, consumers, or the real economy if they cease to be provided or become unreliable. The definition is subject to stakeholder input on scope and demarcation with other activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines benchmark services relevant for DTIA when assessing cross-border benchmark data flows and market impacts.

sections.summary: The Authority may grant licenses under this Act upon application. This section establishes the Authority's power to issue licenses for financial institutions, facilitating regulatory oversight and compliance.

sections.title: Debt collection activities and scope definition
sections.summary: This section outlines the need for a definition of debt collection activities, specifying that it refers to debts from credit agreements under the National Credit Act. It invites stakeholder input on whether debt collection should be a separate activity or integrated with other financial activities like trading. The section emphasizes developing a clear scope for debt collection as a distinct financial activity regulated by the National Credit Regulator.

sections.title: Licensing qualification and retention requirements
sections.summary: Applicants must meet fit and proper requirements, demonstrate business sustainability, operational capabilities, and compliance with financial sector laws. Licensees must continuously comply with licensing requirements to retain their license. The Authority may require applicants to demonstrate they will not conflict with financial customers' interests or the public interest.
sections.dtia_summary: Licensing requirements ensure that financial institutions handling data are properly vetted, which impacts data transfer compliance by requiring institutional legitimacy before data processing occurs.

Institutional forms not afforded legal status by other Acts are granted legal status through inclusion in Schedule 3.

sections.summary: Applications must include information demonstrating compliance with licensing requirements, business sustainability, and operational capabilities. Licensees must maintain license status in documentation and advertisements. Licensees must inform customers of license suspension or withdrawal and remove references to license status from materials.

sections.title: Licensing conditions for financial institutions
sections.summary: The Authority can specify activity, financial product, and customer limitations for financial institutions in licensing conditions. It cannot impose general conditions for activity categories or subcategories. The Authority may set conditions to promote licensee development, financial inclusion, and innovation through technology and practices. It can also impose conditions on business arrangements, persons with whom the licensee conducts business, business scope, contractual terms, fee limits, corporate governance, and fair customer treatment.
sections.dtia_summary: Licensing conditions can restrict cross-border data transfers by limiting financial products, customers, or business arrangements that may involve international data flows.

sections.title: Amendment of licensing conditions
sections.summary: The Authority can amend, delete, replace, or vary licensing conditions on application by a licensee, in the public interest, for financial customers' interests, when revoking a license suspension, to develop the licensee, facilitate financial inclusion, promote innovation, or ensure progressive realization of conditions. Amendments may be temporary with conditions, and if a variation removes a licensee's license for an activity, the licensee must arrange for discharge of obligations, orderly resolution, or transfer of business to another licensed entity.
sections.dtia_summary: Amendments to licensing conditions can affect data transfer by restricting financial products, customers, or business arrangements that may involve international data flows.

sections.summary: A person may not provide activities listed in Schedule 5 or act as a representative unless appointed by the licensee and registered with the Authority. The Authority may prohibit representatives from acting for more than one licensee or providing certain financial services.

sections.title: Representatives appointment criteria
sections.summary: A licensee may only appoint a person as a representative if they are competent, comply with fit and proper standards, governance requirements, operational ability, and capital requirements. The appointment must not materially increase risks, impair governance, compromise customer treatment, prevent best interest actions, or remove key decision-making responsibilities. sections.title: Debarment of financial representatives: conditions and procedures
sections.summary: Financial licensees must debar representatives who no longer meet conduct standards or have materially violated financial laws. Debarment requires written notice, opportunity to respond, and notification of the decision. Licensees must also withdraw authority, remove names from registers, protect client interests, and notify the regulator within five days. Debarment must be initiated within six months of the representative ceasing to be employed. The Authority may require additional information for record-keeping and public registration.
sections.dtia_summary: Debarment procedures require written notice, response opportunities, and client protection measures before transferring or storing data about debarred individuals across borders. The regulator's role in maintaining a public register affects data residency and transparency.

sections.title: Authority's power to prescribe licensing conduct standards
sections.summary: The Financial Sector Regulation Authority may prescribe licensing requirements for conduct standards, including specific categories of licensees, requirements for licensees, transitional matters, representative debarment procedures, fit and proper requirements, governance, operational capital, and local presence mandates. The Authority may also amend fit and proper requirements and require compliance within specified periods.
sections.dtia_summary: Licensing standards affect data handling by financial institutions, particularly regarding client data residency and cross-border transfers of debarred individuals' information. The Authority's power to set standards impacts data governance and compliance.

requirements.text: The Authority may prescribe licensing requirements in conduct standards for matters referred to in this Chapter
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for requirements for licensing, including general and specific category requirements
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for requirements for licensees
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for transitional matters in relation to licensing
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for requirements relating to representatives, including debarment and reappointment
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for fit and proper requirements
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for governance requirements at licensing
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for threshold operational ability requirements
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for threshold operational capital requirements
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for legal status, institutional and structural form
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for naming conventions
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for local presence requirements in South Africa
The Authority may prescribe requirements for activities requiring authority or notification
Different requirements may be prescribed for natural persons and non-natural persons for fit and proper standards
The Authority may amend fit and proper requirements from time to time, and persons must comply within the prescribed period

sections.source_quote: The Authority may prescribe licensing requirements in conduct standards in relation to matters referred to in this Chapter.
sections.relevance_reason: Licensing standards affect data handling but not direct data protection rules

sections.title: Governance requirements for financial institutions
sections.summary: This Chapter establishes governance requirements to ensure fair treatment of financial customers, improve sector confidence, promote appropriate financial products, and enhance market transparency. Financial institutions must prioritize fair outcomes for customers, conduct business with integrity, and maintain adequate resources while avoiding conflicts of interest. The governing body must ensure internal compliance with these requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Governance standards directly impact data handling practices, including client data protection and cross-border data transfers. The emphasis on fair treatment affects data sharing and storage protocols.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must conduct business in a manner that prioritises fair outcomes for financial customers
Financial institutions must conduct business with integrity
Financial institutions must conduct business honestly, fairly, with due skill, care and diligence, and in the best interests of financial customers
Financial institutions must organise and control affairs responsibly and effectively
Financial institutions must maintain adequate financial and other resources
Financial institutions must avoid or manage conflicts of interest
Financial institutions must deal with the Authority in an open and cooperative manner
Financial institutions must comply with conduct standards issued under this Act
Financial institutions must have due regard to the interests and fair treatment of financial customers
Financial institutions must ensure their governing body is accountable for compliance with this Act

sections.source_quote: The purpose of this Chapter and any conduct standards prescribed under this Chapter is to set out governance requirements for financial institutions, so that financial customers can be confident that they are dealing with firms and persons where the fair treatment of customers is central to the corporate culture
sections.relevance_reason: Governance for financial data compliance

sections.title: Corporate culture and fair customer treatment obligations
sections.summary: Financial institutions must prioritize fair treatment of customers as part of their corporate culture. This includes conducting business with integrity, honesty, and due skill and care; maintaining adequate resources; managing conflicts of interest; cooperating with regulators; complying with conduct standards; and ensuring the governing body is accountable for compliance. These obligations apply to all customer interactions.
sections.dtia_summary: These governance standards directly affect how financial institutions handle customer data, including cross-border transfers and storage. The emphasis on fair treatment impacts data sharing and retention policies.

requirements.text: A financial institution must conduct its business in a manner that prioritises fair outcomes for financial customers
A financial institution must conduct business with integrity
A financial institution must conduct business honestly, fairly, with due skill, care and diligence, and in the best interests of financial customers
A financial institution must organise and control its affairs responsibly and effectively
A financial institution must maintain adequate financial and other resources
A financial institution must avoid or manage conflicts of interest
A financial institution must deal with the Authority in an open and cooperative manner
A financial institution must comply with conduct standards issued under this Act
A financial institution must have due regard to the interests and fair treatment of financial customers
A financial institution must ensure its governing body is accountable for compliance with this Act

sections.source_quote: A financial institution must at all times conduct its business in a manner that prioritises fair outcomes for financial customers, so that there is confidence that their fair treatment is central to the corporate culture of the financial institution.
sections.relevance_reason: Supporting governance for financial compliance

sections.heading: The governing body of a financial institution must—
sections.title: Governing body responsibilities for governance
sections.summary: The governing body of a financial institution must endorse and be ultimately responsible for establishing, implementing, reviewing, and ensuring continued internal compliance with governance arrangements. These arrangements must be appropriately embedded within the financial institution to meet the requirements of this Chapter.
sections.dtia_summary: Governing body responsibilities ensure that data handling practices align with governance standards, affecting data storage, sharing, and cross-border transfers. This impacts data residency and compliance with regulatory requirements.

requirements.text: The governing body must endorse and be ultimately responsible for the establishment, implementation, subsequent reviews of, and continued internal compliance with governance arrangements
The governing body must ensure governance arrangements are appropriately embedded in the financial institution

sections.source_quote: The governing body of a financial institution must— endorse and be ultimately responsible for the establishment, implementation, subsequent reviews of, and continued internal compliance with, governance arrangements within the financial institution, to reasonably ensure the achievement of requirements in this Chapter;
sections.relevance_reason: Governing body responsibilities for data governance
regulator powers for financial data

sections.title: Fairness and transparency of financial product terms for retail customers
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure contracts or agreements for retail financial customers are fair, reasonable, and transparent. A term is unfair if it causes significant imbalance in rights, is inequitable to the customer, is not necessary to protect the institution's legitimate interests, causes undue detriment, requires customers to waive rights or assume obligations, or fails to be appropriately disclosed. This applies to both direct retail customers and pension fund members acting on behalf of other retail customers.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to ensure contractual terms are fair and transparent to avoid undue detriment to retail customers during data transfers or processing activities.

requirements.text: A financial institution must ensure that the terms, and conditions of a contract or agreement in respect of a financial product or financial service are fair, reasonable and transparent.
A term or condition of a contract or agreement is unfair or unreasonable if it would cause a significant and unreasonable imbalance in the parties' rights and obligations under the contract.
A term or condition of a contract or agreement is unfair or unreasonable if it is so adverse to the retail customer that they are inequitable.
A term or condition of a contract or agreement is unfair or unreasonable if it is not reasonably necessary to protect the legitimate interests of the financial institution.
A term or condition of a contract or agreement is unfair or unreasonable if it would cause undue detriment to a retail financial customer.

A retail financial customer is required to waive rights, assume obligations, or waive obligations of the financial institution on terms that are unfair, unreasonable, or as a condition to entering into a transaction.
A term or condition of a contract or agreement is transparent if it is expressed in reasonably plain language.
A term or condition of a contract or agreement is transparent if it is legible.
A term or condition of a contract or agreement is transparent if it is presented clearly and unambiguously.
A term or condition of a contract or agreement is transparent if it is readily available to any party affected by the term.

sections.source_quote: A financial institution that provides financial products or financial services to retail financial customers— must ensure that the terms, and conditions of a contract or agreement in respect of a financial product or financial service are fair, reasonable and transparent.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: general contract fairness, not specific data handling

sections.heading: A financial institution may not disclose or use any personal or confidential
sections.title: Confidentiality obligations for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions may not disclose or use personal or confidential information obtained from financial customers except as permitted by the Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013. This section establishes a baseline confidentiality obligation that applies to all financial institutions handling customer data.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to maintain confidentiality of customer data, which impacts data storage and transfer practices across borders.

requirements.text: A financial institution may not disclose or use any personal or confidential information acquired or obtained from a financial customer, except in accordance with the Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013.

sections.source_quote: A financial institution may not disclose or use any personal or confidential information acquired or obtained from a financial customer, except in accordance with the Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 ().
sections.relevance_reason: Confidentiality obligations for financial data

sections.heading: The purpose of this Part is to set out the regulatory framework relating to
sections.title: Purpose of governance policy framework
sections.summary: This section outlines the purpose of Part 2, which establishes the regulatory framework for financial institution governance, including corporate governance, risk management, and internal controls. It sets the foundation for the governance policies required under Section 36.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines the scope of governance policies that financial institutions must implement, which affects data handling practices and compliance with data transfer requirements.
sections.source_quote: The purpose of this Part is to set out the regulatory framework relating to the overall governance of financial institutions, including corporate governance, risk management and internal controls.
sections.relevance_reason: tangential governance for compliance

sections.title: Governance policy implementation requirements
sections.summary: Financial institutions must adopt, document, implement, and monitor a governance policy that ensures adherence to principles in section 30. The policy must be approved by the governing body, be proportionate to the institution's activities and risks, address roles and responsibilities, remuneration practices, record keeping, communication with regulators, management procedures, and compliance procedures. The policy must also include transformation and conflict of interest policies and be reviewed periodically.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to implement comprehensive governance policies that impact data handling, storage, and transfer practices across borders.

requirements.text: A financial institution must adopt, document, implement and monitor the effectiveness of a governance policy that is reasonably expected to ensure adherence to the principles in section 30.
The governance policy must be approved by and subject to the oversight of the governing body.
The governance policy must be proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the activity or activities and the risks of the financial institution.

The governance policy must demonstrate how the financial institution will comply with this Chapter and standards in terms of this Chapter, and must at least address roles and responsibility of the governing body and key persons.
The governance policy must address remuneration and compensation practices.
The governance policy must address record keeping.
The governance policy must address communication with the Authority.
The governance policy must address management procedures.
The governance policy must address compliance procedures.
The governance policy must include the transformation policy required in Part 3 of this Chapter.
The governance policy must include the conflict of interest policy required in Part 4 of this Chapter.
The governance policy must include financial product oversight arrangements required in Chapter 3.
The governance policy must include processes and procedures for the approval of promotional and marketing material required in Part 1 of Chapter 5.
The governance policy must address, and provide for, prescribed matters.

The governance policy, and other policies referred to in subsection (2)(d) and procedures, processes and oversight arrangements that financial institutions are required to have in terms of this Act, must be reviewed and updated from time to time.

sections.source_quote: A financial institution must adopt, document, implement and monitor the effectiveness of a governance policy that is reasonably expected to ensure adherence to the principles in section 30.
sections.relevance_reason: Record keeping and governance policy for financial institutions

sections.heading: The purpose of this Part is to set out the role of a transformation policy in
sections.title: Purpose of transformation policy
sections.summary: This section outlines the purpose of Part 3, which establishes the role of a transformation policy in the governance of financial institutions. It provides context for the transformation policy requirements detailed in subsequent sections.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines the scope of transformation policies that financial institutions must implement, which affects data handling and processing practices.
sections.source_quote: The purpose of this Part is to set out the role of a transformation policy in the governance of a financial institution.
sections.relevance_reason: Governance only, no data compliance relevance

sections.title: Fit and proper requirements for key persons
sections.summary: Key persons and significant owners must comply with prescribed fit and proper requirements. The Authority may verify information through credit bureaus or other sources, direct financial institutions to address non-compliance through education, training, outsourcing, or suspension/removal of key persons, and impose reporting requirements, vary licensing conditions, or suspend licenses if non-compliance occurs.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to ensure key persons comply with fit and proper requirements, which impacts data handling and transfer practices with third parties.

requirements.text: Key persons and significant owners must, at all times, comply with the prescribed fit and proper requirements.
The Authority may request verification of information or verify information by making enquiries to any organ of state, credit bureau or other source of relevant information.
The Authority may direct the financial institution to make arrangements that are satisfactory to the Authority to address non-compliance within a period or subject to conditions.
Arrangements to address non-compliance may include providing additional education or training to the key person.
Arrangements to address non-compliance may include utilising external resources to support the key person.
Arrangements to address non-compliance may include outsourcing the functions and duties of the key person.
Arrangements to address non-compliance may include suspending or removing a person from the appointment as a key person.
If a financial institution fails to make arrangements to address non-compliance, the Authority may impose additional reporting requirements.
If a financial institution fails to make arrangements to address non-compliance, the Authority may vary the financial institution's licensing conditions.
If a financial institution fails to make arrangements to address non-compliance, the Authority may suspend or withdraw the financial institution's licence.

sections.source_quote: Key persons and significant owners must, at all times, comply with the prescribed fit and proper requirements.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for compliance with data governance

sections.heading: The purpose of this Part is to set out the regulatory framework relating to
sections.title: Conflict of interest policy framework for fair customer treatment
sections.summary: This section establishes a regulatory framework to prevent unfair treatment of financial customers by addressing conflicts of interest. Financial institutions must implement policies that ensure fair customer treatment and mitigate risks associated with conflicts of interest. The framework requires documented policies and monitoring processes to assess effectiveness and address potential risks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates conflict of interest policies that must be proportionate to the institution's activities and risks, ensuring fair customer treatment. It directly impacts data transfer assessments by requiring clear disclosure mechanisms for conflicts involving related parties.

requirements.text: A financial institution must have a documented conflict of interest policy to promote effective oversight of conflicts of interest
A financial institution must have an objective monitoring and compliance process for implementing the policy
A financial institution must have an objective monitoring and compliance process for assessing policy effectiveness

sections.source_quote: The purpose of this Part is to set out the regulatory framework relating to the avoidance or mitigation of conflicts of interest that pose a risk of unfair treatment of financial customers.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory framework for conflict of interest policies in financial institutions

sections.title: Conflict of interest policy requirements for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must maintain a documented conflict of interest policy that is proportionate to their activities and risks. The policy must clearly define where conflicts arise, specify roles and responsibilities, require disclosure to customers and governing bodies, and include corrective actions for non-compliance. It must also ensure transactions with related parties are conducted on an arm's length basis.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires conflict of interest policies that must be proportionate to the institution's activities and risks, ensuring fair customer treatment. It directly impacts data transfer assessments by mandating disclosure mechanisms for conflicts involving related parties.

requirements.text: A conflict of interest policy must be proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the activity or activities and the risks of the financial institution
A conflict of interest policy must clearly define where conflicts of interest may arise
A conflict of interest policy must define the roles and responsibilities of persons accountable for the management and oversight of conflicts of interest
A conflict of interest policy must set requirements for how conflicts of interest must be disclosed and documented to the governing body, senior management and financial customers
A conflict of interest policy must provide for corrective actions that must be taken for non-compliance with the policy
A conflict of interest policy must provide for adequate processes and procedures for transactions with related parties to ensure they are made on an arm's length basis

sections.source_quote: A conflict of interest policy must— (a) be proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the activity or activities and the risks of the financial institution; (b) clearly define where conflicts of interest may arise; (c) define the roles and responsibilities of persons accountable for the management and oversight of conflicts of interest; (d) set requirements relating to how conflicts of interest must be disclosed and documented to the governing body, senior management and financial customers; (e) provide for corrective actions that must be taken for non-compliance with the policy; (f) provide for adequate processes and procedures for transactions with related parties, so that they are made on an arm's length basis; and (g) address, and provide for, prescribed matters.
sections.relevance_reason: Supporting: conflict policy for financial data

sections.heading: A financial institution, at the request of the Authority, must provide the
sections.title: Disclosure of related parties to the Authority
sections.summary: Financial institutions must provide the Authority with information about related and inter-related parties, joint ventures, partnerships, or intended partnerships upon request. This requirement ensures transparency in financial relationships and helps prevent conflicts of interest that could affect customer treatment.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires financial institutions to disclose related parties to the Authority, which is critical for data transfer assessments to identify potential conflicts of interest and ensure transparency in financial relationships.

requirements.text: A financial institution, at the request of the Authority, must provide the Authority with information relating to the financial institution's related and inter-related parties, or any joint ventures or partnerships that the financial institution participates in, or intends to enter into

sections.source_quote: A financial institution, at the request of the Authority, must provide the Authority with information relating to the financial institution's related and inter-related parties, or any joint ventures or partnerships that the financial institution participates in, or intends to enter into.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers and transparency for financial data

sections.heading: The purpose of this Part is to ensure fair remuneration and compensation
sections.title: Fair remuneration and compensation principles
sections.summary: This section ensures fair remuneration and compensation practices between financial institutions and service providers that benefit financial customers and avoid conflicts of interest. It requires remuneration practices that are in the interests of customers and do not create unfair outcomes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates fair remuneration practices that must be in the interests of financial customers and avoid conflicts of interest, directly impacting data transfer assessments by ensuring remuneration structures do not create unfair treatment of customers.

requirements.text: The purpose of this Part is to ensure fair remuneration and compensation practices between financial institutions and persons providing activities or services to financial institutions, including representatives, contractors and service providers, that are in the interests of financial customers

The purpose of this Part is to ensure fair remuneration and compensation practices between financial institutions and persons providing activities or services to financial institutions, including representatives, contractors and service providers, that seek to avoid conflicts of interest

sections.source_quote: The purpose of this Part is to ensure fair remuneration and compensation practices between financial institutions and persons providing activities or services to financial institutions, including representatives, contractors and service providers, that— (a) are in the interests of financial customers; and (b) seek to avoid conflicts of interest.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial regulation, no data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.title: Remuneration and compensation standards
sections.summary: Remuneration, compensation, and consideration must be reasonably commensurate with the activity or service performed, not result in double remuneration, and not be structured in a way that increases the risk of unfair outcomes for financial customers. This section applies to remuneration provided by financial institutions or their associates and accepted by service providers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires remuneration practices to be reasonably commensurate with services, avoid double payment, and not create unfair outcomes for customers, directly impacting data transfer assessments by ensuring remuneration structures do not create conflicts of interest.

requirements.text: Remuneration, compensation or consideration paid to, or received by, any person for the rendering of any activity or service performed by that person must be reasonably commensurate with the actual activity or service performed
Remuneration, compensation or consideration must not result in any activity or service being remunerated twice
Remuneration, compensation or consideration must not be structured in a manner that may increase the risk of unfair outcomes for financial customers

sections.source_quote: Remuneration, compensation or consideration referred to in subsection (1) that is paid to, or received by, any person referred to in subsection (1) for the rendering of any activity or service performed by that person, must— (a) be reasonably commensurate with the actual activity or service performed; (b) not result in any activity or service being remunerated twice; and (c) not be structured in a manner that may increase the risk of unfair outcomes for financial customers.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: general financial governance

sections.title: Conduct standards for governance and culture
sections.summary: The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for governance, risk management, compliance, internal controls, outsourcing, record keeping, and other areas to ensure financial institutions operate effectively and ethically. These standards can apply to small enterprises and may impose stricter requirements than other acts.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows the Authority to prescribe conduct standards for governance, risk management, and internal controls, which directly impact data transfer assessments by requiring robust governance frameworks and oversight mechanisms.

requirements.text: The Authority may prescribe conduct standards that provide for requirements, limitations or prohibitions on matters referred to in this Chapter
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for small enterprises that are applicable to matters in Parts 2, 3 and 5
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards in respect of committees or other structures that a financial institution must establish
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for the governing body of the financial institution, including composition, governance, roles and responsibilities
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for risk management, including risk management systems, strategies, policies and own risk assessments
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for compliance, including compliance systems, strategies and policies
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for internal controls, including internal control systems
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for control functions, including required control functions, requirements for control functions and roles
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for outsourcing arrangements, including outsourcing policies, principles, requirements, and functions that may not be outsourced
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for record keeping, data management and protection of financial customer information

The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for governance policy, including roles and responsibilities, decision-making processes, remuneration practices, communication with the Authority, and corrective actions
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for transformation policy
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for fit and proper requirements in respect of key persons and significant owners
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for avoiding or mitigating conflict of interest, including business practices, ownership arrangements, and financial services
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for remuneration and compensation, including prohibitions on remuneration, timing and conditions for remuneration, and adjustments, refunds or claw backs
The Authority may prescribe conduct standards for prohibited practices and unfair practices

sections.source_quote: The Authority may prescribe conduct standards that provide for requirements, limitations or prohibitions on matters referred to in this Chapter.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for data governance standards

sections.heading: The purpose of this Chapter and any conduct standards prescribed under
sections.title: Purpose of Chapter and conduct standards for financial products
sections.summary: This section establishes the purpose of the Chapter and any conduct standards to ensure financial products are appropriate for targeted customer needs while facilitating efficiency, flexibility, and innovation in financial services. It sets the foundation for product design and suitability standards that must align with customer expectations and market conditions. The standards apply to financial product providers and their customers.

requirements.text: The purpose of this Chapter and any conduct standards prescribed under this Chapter is to promote the supply to financial customers of financial products that are appropriate in relation to targeted customer needs, circumstances and expectations

sections.source_quote: The purpose of this Chapter and any conduct standards prescribed under this Chapter is to promote the supply to financial customers of financial products that are appropriate in relation to targeted customer needs, circumstances and expectations, while facilitating efficiency, flexibility and innovation in the provision of financial products.
sections.relevance_reason: General governance for financial products

sections.title: Financial products must meet customer needs and expectations
sections.summary: Financial products must be designed to meet the needs of identified customer groups and perform as expected based on information provided. Providers must ensure their staff have the necessary skills to understand product features and customer needs. This section mandates that products align with customer expectations and that design processes consider target market characteristics.

requirements.text: Financial products must be designed with due regard to the interest of financial customers and, in the case of retail financial customers, must be designed to meet the needs of identified groups of financial customers and must be targeted accordingly

A financial product provider must ensure that their financial customers are provided with products that perform as that provider has led its customers to expect, through the information, representations and advertising provided by or on behalf of the financial product provider

A financial product provider must ensure that relevant personnel involved in designing a financial product possess the necessary skills, knowledge and expertise to properly understand the financial product's main features and characteristics, as well as the interests, objectives and characteristics of the target market

sections.source_quote: Financial products must be designed with due regard to the interest of financial customers and, in the case of retail financial customers, must be designed to meet the needs of identified groups of financial customers and must be targeted accordingly. A financial product provider must ensure that their financial customers are provided with products that perform as that provider has led its customers to expect, through the information, representations and advertising provided by or on behalf of the financial product provider. A financial product provider must ensure that relevant personnel involved in designing a financial product possess the necessary skills, knowledge and expertise to properly understand the financial product's main features and characteristics, as well as the interests, objectives and characteristics of the target market.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: general governance for financial products

sections.title: Product oversight arrangements for financial products
sections.summary: Financial product providers must establish oversight arrangements to monitor product design processes and address products that could lead to poor customer outcomes. These arrangements must support compliance with conduct standards, manage conflicts of interest, account for customer risks, define roles, and include senior management confirmation. Small enterprises have fewer requirements, but must still maintain robust oversight.

requirements.text: A financial product provider must establish and implement product oversight arrangements relating to the design of financial products, to— monitor and review the design process and procedures on an on-going basis

A financial product provider must establish and implement product oversight arrangements relating to the design of financial products, to— ensure that remedial action is taken in respect of financial products that are reasonably expected to lead, or are leading, to poor or unfair outcomes for financial customers
The financial product oversight arrangements must support the achievement of sections 46 and 47, and provide for senior management confirmation in this regard

The financial product oversight arrangements must support the proper management of conflicts of interest, and ensure that the objectives, interests and characteristics of targeted financial customers are duly taken into account
The financial product oversight arrangements must be appropriate to account for risks borne by financial customers or groups of financial customers for a financial product

The financial product oversight arrangements must allocate clear roles and responsibilities for persons in the financial institution responsible for, or partly responsible for, establishing and implementing the oversight arrangements
The financial product oversight arrangements must incorporate effective assessment by the risk and compliance functions of the extent to which sections 46 and 47 are being achieved

The financial product oversight arrangements must include senior management confirmation that a product adequately meets required outcomes for the fair treatment of financial customers, including the requirement that it will perform as financial customers are led to expect
The financial product oversight arrangements must include appropriate measures and procedures to ensure the financial institution's compliance with this Chapter

The financial product oversight arrangements may vary depending on the financial product, in accordance with the principle of proportionality, taking into consideration the nature, scale and complexity of the relevant business of the financial institution and the complexity of the financial product, and that financial institution's business model

A financial product provider that is not a small enterprise must include in its product oversight arrangements— a product design policy, and ensure that the policy is not compromised as a result of commercial, time or funding pressures
A financial product provider that is not a small enterprise must include in its product oversight arrangements— a product approval process
Financial product provider referred to in subsection (4) must review and update the approval process on a regular basis to ensure that it remains robust and fit for purpose

A financial product provider that is not a small enterprise must set out the financial product oversight arrangements as part of the governance policy specified in section 36 and make it available to relevant persons involved in product design, including staff members

A financial institution that provides a financial product must regularly review the product oversight arrangements to ensure that these remain valid and up to date, and must amend the governance policy where appropriate

A new financial product must be signed off by the governing body before a financial institution that provides a financial product starts to market offer or enter into contracts in respect of the product

The sign-off of a new financial product by the governing body must be accompanied by a confirmation that the financial product, distribution methods and disclosure documents meet the requirements set out in subsection (2)

sections.source_quote: A financial product provider must establish and implement product oversight arrangements relating to the design of financial products, to— monitor and review the design process and procedures on an on-going basis; and ensure that remedial action is taken in respect of financial products that are reasonably expected to lead, or are leading, to poor or unfair outcomes for financial customers.
sections.relevance_reason: Supporting: oversight arrangements for financial products

sections.title: Design and suitability for retail financial customers
sections.summary: Financial institutions must assess retail customer needs, ensure products align with business models and risk management, target appropriate customer groups, and adjust products to meet changing needs. This section applies to pension funds and similar entities, requiring the same standards for their members.

requirements.text: A financial institution that provides financial products to retail customers must, when developing the product, make use of adequate information on retail financial customers' needs and undertake a thorough assessment of the main characteristics of a new product, the distribution methods intended to be used in relation to the product, and the related disclosure documents, by competent persons with relevant skills to ensure that the product, distribution methods and disclosure documents— are consistent with the financial institution's business model, risk management approach and applicable conduct standards

A financial institution that provides financial products to retail customers must, when developing the product, make use of adequate information on retail financial customers' needs and undertake a thorough assessment of the main characteristics of a new product, the distribution methods intended to be used in relation to the product, and the related disclosure documents, by competent persons with relevant skills to ensure that the product, distribution methods and disclosure documents— target the retail financial customers for whose needs the product is likely to be appropriate, while taking reasonable measures to limit access by retail financial customers for whom the product is likely to be inappropriate

A financial institution that provides financial products to retail customers must, when developing the product, make use of adequate information on retail financial customers' needs and undertake a thorough assessment of the main characteristics of a new product, the distribution methods intended to be used in relation to the product, and the related disclosure documents, by competent persons with relevant skills to ensure that the product, distribution methods and disclosure documents— take into account the risks resulting or that may result from the product that could harm retail financial customers

A financial institution that provides financial products to retail customers must, when developing the product, make use of adequate information on retail financial customers' needs and undertake a thorough assessment of the main characteristics of a new product, the distribution methods intended to be used in relation to the product, and the related disclosure documents, by competent persons with relevant skills to ensure that the product, distribution methods and disclosure documents— are appropriate, taking into account considerations of fairness

A financial institution that provides financial products to retail customers must take appropriate steps to identify the needs of the group or groups of financial customers to whom its products are targeted and to satisfy itself, prior to making the financial products available to the market, and on an on-going basis, that the financial products are appropriate to meet those needs

A financial institution that provides financial products to retail customers must ensure that financial product design provides sufficient flexibility to cope with reasonably expected changes in a financial customer's needs during the lifetime of the product

A financial institution that provides financial products to retail customers must adjust the design of the financial products that it provides to— respond to identified changes that affect the financial products and impact on financial customers

A financial institution that provides financial products to retail customers must adjust the design of the financial products that it provides to— allow sufficient flexibility to meet financial customers' changing needs

sections.source_quote: A financial institution that provides financial products to retail customers must— when developing the product, make use of adequate information on retail financial customers' needs and undertake a thorough assessment of the main characteristics of a new product, the distribution methods intended to be used in relation to the product, and the related disclosure documents, by competent persons with relevant skills to ensure that the product, distribution methods and disclosure documents— are consistent with the financial institution's business model, risk management approach and applicable conduct standards; target the retail financial customers for whose needs the product is likely to be appropriate, while taking reasonable measures to limit access by retail financial customers for whom the product is likely to be inappropriate; take into account the risks resulting or that may result from the product that could harm retail financial customers; are appropriate, taking into account considerations of fairness;
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data handling and customer needs assessment

sections.heading: Products provided to financial customers must—
sections.title: Product performance and post-sales service
sections.summary: Financial products must undergo ongoing monitoring and reporting to enable informed customer decisions, meet reasonable expectations, and provide adequate post-sales service. This ensures products remain aligned with customer needs and expectations throughout their lifecycle.

requirements.text: Products provided to financial customers must be subject to on-going monitoring and periodic reporting of product performance, to allow financial customers to make on-going, informed decisions in respect of financial products
Products provided to financial customers must meet the reasonable expectations created with financial customers
Products provided to financial customers must be supported by adequate post-sales service

sections.source_quote: Products provided to financial customers must— be subject to on-going monitoring and periodic reporting of product performance, to allow financial customers to make on-going, informed decisions in respect of financial products; meet the reasonable expectations created with financial customers; and be supported by adequate post-sales service.

sections.title: Statutory manager appointment for insurers
sections.summary: This section allows the Prudential Authority to appoint a statutory manager for an insurer or controlling company without court intervention. The appointment takes effect immediately but requires court confirmation within 30 days. The statutory manager gains full access to financial records, participates in management, has final decision authority in disputes, and receives court-ordered remuneration. The statutory manager must manage affairs economically, report to the Prudential Authority, and address concerns that led to appointment. If steps are impractical, the statutory manager must propose business transfer or winding-up. The statutory manager must comply with Prudential Authority directives and may apply to court for directions. The Prudential Authority may terminate or replace the statutory manager. The statutory manager is not liable for losses unless due to fraud, dishonesty, or legal non-compliance. This provision does not limit the Prudential Authority's other powers. No business rescue proceedings may commence until termination of the statutory manager.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a statutory manager mechanism for insurers, requiring immediate appointment with court confirmation within 30 days. The statutory manager must access financial records, participate in management, and address concerns that led to appointment. If steps to resolve issues are impractical, the statutory manager must propose business transfer or winding-up. This impacts data transfer DTIAs by creating a governance structure for financial institutions during insolvency or crisis.

sections.title: Oversight arrangements for financial services
sections.summary: Financial service providers must establish ongoing oversight arrangements that support conduct standards, manage conflicts of interest, identify risks to customers, allocate roles, assess compliance, and ensure proportionality based on business scale. These arrangements must be reviewed regularly and documented for larger institutions.

requirements.text: Establish and implement, on an on-going basis, oversight arrangements relating to the provision of financial services.
Support the achievement of sections 52 and 53, and provide for senior management confirmation in this regard.
Ensure proper disclosure and management of conflicts of interest, and ensure that the objectives, interests and characteristics of targeted financial customers are duly taken into account.
Identify and disclose risks that financial customers or groups of financial customers for a financial service is exposed to.
Allocate clear roles and responsibilities for persons in the financial institution responsible for, or partly responsible for, establishing and implementing the oversight arrangements.
Incorporate effective assessment by the risk and compliance functions of the extent to which sections 52 and 53 are being achieved.
Include appropriate measures and procedures to ensure the financial institution's compliance with this Chapter.

Oversight arrangements may vary depending on the financial service in accordance with the principle of proportionality, taking into consideration the nature, scale and complexity of the relevant business of the financial institution, and that financial institution's business model.

A financial service provider that is not a small enterprise must set out the service oversight arrangements as part of the governance policy specified in section 36 and make it available to relevant persons.
Regularly review the service oversight arrangements to ensure that these remain valid and up to date, and must amend the governance policy, where applicable and where appropriate.
A new financial service must be signed off by the governing body of a financial institution before the financial institution markets, offers or enters into contracts to provide the financial service.
The sign-off of a new financial service by the governing body referred to in paragraph (a) must be accompanied by a confirmation that the service reasonably complies with subsection (2).

sections.source_quote: A financial service provider must establish and implement, on an on-going basis, oversight arrangements relating to the provision of financial services. The oversight arrangements referred to in (1) must— (a) support the achievement of sections 52 and 53, and provide for senior management confirmation in this regard; (b) ensure proper disclosure and management of conflicts of interest, and ensure that the objectives, interests and characteristics of targeted financial customers are duly taken into account; (c) be appropriate to identify and disclose risks that financial customers or groups of financial customers for a financial service is exposed to; (d) allocate clear roles and responsibilities for persons in the financial institution responsible for, or partly responsible for, establishing and implementing the oversight arrangements; (e) incorporate effective assessment by the risk and compliance functions of the extent to which sections 52 and 53 are being achieved; and (f) include appropriate measures and procedures to ensure the financial institution's compliance with this Chapter.

sections.title: Retail financial customers and service assessment requirements
sections.summary: Financial institutions providing retail services must assess service characteristics and disclosure documents to ensure consistency with business models and risk management, and mitigate risks to customers. requirements.text: Conduct a thorough assessment of the main characteristics of the financial service, and related disclosure documents, to ensure that the service and disclosure documents are consistent with the financial institution's business model, risk management approach and applicable conduct standards.
Take steps to mitigate risks resulting or that may result from the service that could harm retail financial customers.

On an on-going basis take into account changes in customer needs, circumstances or expectations, or external factors such as economic conditions, that could affect how the financial service impacts on financial customers.
Allow sufficient flexibility in the way in which the service is provided to meet these changes without harm or prejudice to the customer.

sections.source_quote: A financial institution that provides financial services to retail financial customers must— (a) conduct a thorough assessment of the main characteristics of the financial service, and related disclosure documents, to ensure that the service and disclosure documents— (i) are consistent with the financial institution's business model, risk management approach and applicable conduct standards; and (ii) take steps to mitigate risks resulting or that may result from the service that could harm retail financial customers; and (b) on an on-going basis take into account changes in customer needs, circumstances or expectations, or external factors such as economic conditions, that could affect how the financial service impacts on financial customers, and allow sufficient flexibility in the way in which the service is provided to meet these changes without harm or prejudice to the customer.

sections.summary: Financial institutions must promote financial products and services using clear, fair, unambiguous language that does not mislead customers. (1) A financial institution must ensure that financial products and financial services are promoted and marketed to financial customers in a way that is clear, fair, unambiguous and not misleading. (2) Before, during and after the conclusion of a contract or agreement for the provision of a financial product or a financial service or the participation in a product, a financial customer must be given adequate and clear information, and be kept appropriately informed, to place the financial customer in a position to make informed decisions regarding the financial product or financial service, including whether the financial product or financial service meets the financial customer's needs and expectations.

sections.source_quote: 59. (1) A financial institution must, prior to publishing promotional and marketing material, take reasonable measures to ensure that the information provided in the promotional and marketing material complies with this Chapter and conduct standards prescribed under this Chapter. (2) A financial institution must at all times ensure that any publication of promotional and marketing material which relates to its business, activities, financial products or financial services, that another person publishes on behalf of the financial institution, or of which the financial institution is aware or ought to be aware, complies with this Chapter and conduct standards prescribed under this Chapter. (3) A financial institution remains responsible for the manner in which a financial product issued by it or a financial service rendered by it is promoted or marketed, even where the financial institution relies on another person to promote or market the financial product or service on its behalf. (4) Promotional and marketing material of a financial institution must be appropriate to the needs and reasonably assumed level of knowledge of the financial customers at whom it is targeted. (5) Promotional and marketing material of a financial institution may not be misleading or likely to mislead, deceptive, fraudulent, contrary to the public interest or contain incorrect statements. (6) Promotional and marketing material must use clear, plain and unambiguous language, and take into account the needs and reasonably assumed level of knowledge of the retail financial customers to whom it is targeted. (7) A financial institution that is not a small enterprise must have processes and procedures for the approval of promotional and marketing material by a person of appropriate seniority and expertise within the business of the financial institution, which must form part of the governance policy required in section 36(2)(d).

sections.source_quote: 60. (1) Before, during and after the conclusion of a contract for the provision of a financial product or a financial service, a financial institution must make a financial customer aware of all relevant facts that could influence the financial customers' decisions relating to the financial product or financial service, including but not limited to— (a) benefits and risks in relation to the financial product or financial service; (b) all costs to the financial customer in relation to the supply of that product or service; (c) contractual obligations on the financial customer and the financial institution; (d) consequences for each party should there be a breach of contract; and (e) recourse options for the financial customer in the case of a dispute with the financial institution, or a related intermediary, in relation to its supply of a financial product or financial service. (2) A financial institution must make disclosures to financial customers that— (a) use plain language that is clear, unambiguous, and is appropriate for the target market; (b) are adequate, appropriate, timely, relevant and complete; (c) are factually correct and not misleading or deceptive; (d) promote understanding of the financial product or financial service being provided; and (e) promote comparison across similar financial products or financial services. (3) When making disclosures to financial customers in accordance with this Part, a financial institution must take into account— (a) the nature and complexity of the financial product or financial service concerned; and (b) the needs and reasonably assumed level of knowledge, understanding and experience of financial customers at whom the disclosure is targeted.

sections.source_quote: 61. (1) The Authority may prescribe conduct standards providing for additional requirements, limitations or prohibitions on matters referred to in this Chapter. (2) Without limiting the scope of the conduct standards that may be prescribed in terms of subsection (1), the Authority may prescribe conduct standards in respect of— (a) the design of promotional and marketing material; (b) the positioning of words in, and the display and presentation of promotional and marketing material; (c) information, data, descriptions and disclosures in promotional and marketing material; (d) the appropriateness of the medium used for promoting and marketing a financial product or service; (e) the identification of the financial institution that is financially liable or accountable for the financial product or financial service that is being promoted or marketed; (f) direct marketing, product and services descriptions, bait marketing, negative option marketing; (g) inducements; (h) loyalty programmes and competitions; (i) the publication of prices; (j) investment performance and projected values contained in promotional and marketing material; (k) comparative promotional and marketing practices; (l) false, misleading or deceptive representations; (m) third party arrangements; (n) puffery, endorsements and value judgments, matters of opinion or subjective assessments; (o) prohibited and predatory marketing practices; (p) remedial actions to be taken by a financial institution when it has published an inaccurate, unclear, misleading or fraudulent promotional or marketing material; (q) the provision of information in plain and understandable language; (r) standardised point-of-sale disclosure documents, or key information documents, which may be adapted to also provide for the standardisation of disclosure over the life of the financial product or financial service, and which may include information about pricing; (s) disclosures, including— (i) the person responsible for making certain disclosures; (ii) required disclosures; (iii) the appropriateness of certain disclosures; (iv) the content of and accuracy of disclosures; and (t) restrictions on unsolicited communications between a financial institution that provides a financial product or financial service and a financial customer before contracting and once the contractual relationship is terminated; and (u) disclosures in relation to financial products where membership is a requirement of employment.

sections.summary: This Chapter aims to ensure financial products are delivered appropriately to customers, enable customers to understand and compare financial products, enhance professionalism in distribution and advice, promote fair competition, and support sustainable business models for long-term fair customer outcomes.
