requirements.text: The purpose of this Chapter and any conduct standards prescribed under this Chapter is to ensure that the distribution of financial products and financial instruments to financial customers, advice provided in relation to financial products and financial instruments, and discretionary investment management supports the delivery of appropriate financial products and financial instruments to financial customers.

The purpose of this Chapter and any conduct standards prescribed under this Chapter is to enable financial customers to understand and compare the nature, value and cost of financial products and financial instruments.
The purpose of this Chapter and any conduct standards prescribed under this Chapter is to enhance standards of professionalism in distribution, advice and discretionary investment management.

The purpose of this Chapter and any conduct standards prescribed under this Chapter is to enable financial customers and persons that distribute financial products and financial instruments to benefit from fair competition for quality services related to that distribution, at a price more closely aligned with the nature and quality of the service provided.

The purpose of this Chapter and any conduct standards prescribed under this Chapter is to support sustainable business models for distribution, advice and discretionary investment management to viably deliver fair customer outcomes over the long term.

sections.title: Trustee duties for financial customer assets

sections.title: Statutory manager appointment for insurers
sections.summary: This section allows the Prudential Authority to appoint a statutory manager for an insurer or controlling company without court intervention. The appointment takes effect immediately but requires court confirmation within 30 days. The statutory manager gains full access to financial records, participates in management, has final decision authority in disputes, and receives court-ordered remuneration. The statutory manager must manage affairs economically, report to the Prudential Authority, and address concerns that led to appointment. If steps are impractical, the statutory manager must propose business transfer or winding-up. The statutory manager must comply with Prudential Authority directives and may apply to court for directions. The Prudential Authority may terminate or replace the statutory manager. The statutory manager is not liable for losses unless due to fraud, dishonesty, or legal non-compliance. This provision does not limit the Prudential Authority's other powers. No business rescue proceedings may commence until termination of the statutory manager.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a statutory manager mechanism for insurers, requiring immediate appointment with court confirmation within 30 days. The statutory manager must access financial records, participate in management, and address concerns that led to appointment. If steps to resolve issues are impractical, the statutory manager must propose business transfer or winding-up. This impacts data transfer DTIAs by creating a governance structure for financial institutions during insolvency or crisis.

sections.heading: The amendment of section 54—
sections.title: Curator appointment for insurers
sections.summary: This section amends section 54 to allow the Prudential Authority to appoint a curator for an insurer or controlling company without court intervention. The curator may be provisionally appointed with specific conditions and a rule nisi for confirmation. The court may suspend legal proceedings, grant investigation powers, and set remuneration. The curator acts under Prudential Authority control and must report to them. Creditors and clients retain their rights. The appointment lapses if the Prudential Authority withdraws the letter of appointment or if the court cancels it. The curator may apply to the Prudential Authority for instructions.

sections.heading: Distributing
sections.title: Application to public sector pension funds
sections.summary: The Act applies to all public sector pension funds. Unregistered pension funds must apply for registration within 12 months of commencement. Legislation establishing pension funds must be amended within 18 months to align with the Act. Pension fund rules must also be amended within 18 months to align with the Act. New pension funds must comply with the Act and their rules.

sections.heading: Managing and
sections.title: Application of the Act to financial institutions
sections.summary: This section specifies the application of the Financial Conduct of Financial Institutions Bill to financial institutions, including the requirement that financial institutions providing financial products and services must be licensed under this Act. It also states that the Act applies to all supervised entities, including those that are not financial institutions, and imposes requirements relating to the licensing of financial institutions, supervised entities, the licensing of certain persons related to financial institutions, and the financial products and services provided by financial institutions.

sections.heading: Professional
sections.title: Professional custodian services for financial assets
sections.summary: This section defines professional custodian services as holding assets in custody on behalf of financial customers as a business or part of a business. It specifies that this activity relates to the current FAIS 'administrative FSP' (LISP) category where the distinguishing feature is 'bulking', but notes that further consideration is being given to whether other forms of investment platforms require licensing. The Authority has regulatory authority over this activity under section 291 of the Financial Sector Regulation Act.

sections.heading: Payment Service
sections.title: Conversion of previously licensed financial institutions
sections.summary: This section mandates that previously licensed financial institutions continue operating under the previous Act until their licenses are converted to this Act's licenses within two years. The Authority must convert licenses within two years, publish a conversion process within two months, and ensure the conversion process is reasonable and fair with sufficient engagement. It also specifies that institutions must be converted to activities similar to their previous business or make arrangements to discharge obligations, ensure orderly resolution, or transfer business if they do not meet the criteria.

A previously licensed financial institution may only be issued a license for activities it was permitted to conduct under the previous Act

If the Authority does not convert a license for an activity similar to the institution's previous business, the institution must discharge obligations under contracts, ensure orderly resolution, or transfer business to another financial institution

sections.source_quote: As of the effective date of this Schedule, every previously licensed financial institution that was, immediately before that date, licensed under the previous Act continues to exist as a licensed financial institution in terms of the previous Act, as if it had been licensed under this Act, and may continue to conduct the business for which it was licensed, until its licence under the previous Act is converted to a licence under this Act during the period of two years referred to in sub-item (2), subject to and in accordance with the requirements and obligations imposed under this Act.

sections.heading: Financial Markets
sections.title: Underwriting public offerings
sections.summary: This section defines underwriting a public offering as guaranteeing the sale of securities by purchasing them at a stated price from the issuer. It notes that this activity is indirectly regulated under the Financial Markets Act for listed securities and the Companies Act for unlisted securities, with input sought on whether it must be licensed. The underwriting of CIS participatory interests is included in the activity of the provider of a pooled investment.

sections.heading: a.
sections.title: Trading and market activities
sections.summary: This section defines trading as buying or selling securities for own account or on behalf of another person as a business, part of a business, or incidental to conducting a business. It also defines making a market as stating prices at which financial instruments can be acquired or disposed of, and clearing services as calculating and determining exact numbers of securities and money for settlement. Settlement services involve completing transactions through a settlement system. The definitions are subject to stakeholder engagement on scope and demarcation.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines trading and related market activities relevant for DTIA when assessing cross-border securities transactions and data flows.

Settling transactions in securities by effecting transfer of securities and payment of funds through a settlement system

sections.heading: Providing
sections.title: Benchmark and index services
sections.summary: This section defines benchmark services as providing benchmarks used directly or indirectly within financial instruments or contracts, or indices that have no or very few market-led substitutes. It specifies that benchmarks must not cause significant adverse impacts on market integrity, financial stability, consumers, or the real economy if they cease to be provided or become unreliable. The definition is subject to stakeholder input on scope and demarcation with other activities.

sections.heading: Credit Rating
sections.title: Credit rating services
sections.summary: This section defines credit rating services as gathering, collecting, analyzing, evaluating, and issuing opinions on the creditworthiness of entities, securities, financial instruments, or sovereign states using a defined ranking system. It explicitly excludes credit scores, credit scoring systems, and assessments related to consumer, commercial, or industrial relationships.

sections.heading: Debt Collection
sections.title: Debt collection activities and scope definition
sections.summary: This section outlines the need for a definition of debt collection activities, specifying that it refers to debts from credit agreements under the National Credit Act. It invites stakeholder input on whether debt collection should be a separate activity or integrated with other financial activities like trading. The section emphasizes developing a clear scope for debt collection as a distinct financial activity regulated by the National Credit Regulator.
